title: Jerk pork shoulder
subtitle: Slow-roasted pork shoulder with Jamaican jerk seasoning
excerpt: Pork shoulder rubbed in jerk paste and slow-roasted until pull-apart tender with a deeply spiced, charred crust. The long cooking transforms the marinade into a sticky, lacquered surface.
sourceNotes: Jerk pork is the original Jamaican jerk preparation, predating jerk chicken. The Maroons of Jamaica traditionally cooked whole pigs over pimento wood pits, using a spice paste of scotch bonnet, allspice, and thyme. This recipe uses bone-in pork shoulder for its fat content and collagen, which keeps the meat moist through the long cooking. Pork shoulder is the most practical cut for home cooking; a whole pork leg or a butterflied leg are also good candidates for jerk treatment.

content.text: Jerk pork rewards patience more than most dishes. The shoulder needs four hours at low heat before it will pull apart, and the final blast at high temperature lacquers the crust into something dark and sticky. The long marination (overnight is the minimum) is not optional: pork shoulder is dense, and the jerk paste needs time to get past the surface.

Bone-in shoulder is worth seeking out over boneless. The bone conducts heat into the centre of the joint and the fat around it bastes the meat from within as it cooks. When the bone pulls free without resistance and the meat shreds with two forks, the pork is done.

Put the {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnets, {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, {{allspice}} of allspice, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, {{nutmeg-ground}} of nutmeg, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{soy-sauce-dark}} of dark soy sauce, the juice of the {{lime}} limes, and {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into a food processor. Blitz to a rough paste. Score the {{pork-shoulder}} of pork shoulder all over with a sharp knife, cutting 2 cm deep. Rub the paste all over and into the scores. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours, ideally overnight.

Remove the pork from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Place the pork in a deep roasting tin, cover tightly with foil, and roast for 3.5 to 4 hours until the meat is pull-apart tender. Remove the foil, increase the oven to 220°C fan, and roast for a further 20 to 30 minutes until the surface is deeply charred in places and the juices are sticky. Rest for 30 minutes before pulling the meat apart with two forks.

items.fix: Continue cooking covered for another 30 to 45 minutes. A probe thermometer should read at least 90°C in the centre for pull-apart texture. Don't rush, the collagen takes time to break down.
items.cause: Oven temperature too low; joint too large or thick

items.fix: Keep the foil on until the last 20 to 30 minutes. The blast of high heat at the end is what lacquers the crust, do it too long and it burns rather than caramelises.
items.cause: Foil removed too early; oven too hot
items.symptom: Surface burning before internal temperature reaches target

items.fix: Use 1 scotch bonnet, fully deseeded, for the first time you make this. You can always add more heat next time. Serve with coconut rice, which absorbs and softens heat.
items.cause: Too many scotch bonnets; seeds left in
items.symptom: Jerk paste too hot to be edible

items.fix: Bone-in shoulder has more fat and collagen to keep it moist. If using boneless, reduce the cooking time to 3 hours covered and check early.
items.cause: Boneless shoulder; foil not sealed tightly

content.text: Jerk pork comes from the cooking of the Maroons. The Maroons were communities of formerly enslaved people who lived in the mountains of Jamaica. They worked out how to pit-roast whole pigs over pimento wood. The pairing of allspice and scotch bonnet is what gives jerk its identity. Both are native to Jamaica. The pimento tree, which gives allspice, is central to the original method. Its green wood and leaves are used as fuel and as a smoking bed. The home oven version is a fair approximation. The Blue Mountain pit version is a different experience.
